steps.title: Read the setup before the blank
steps.content: The text says the declaration is "explicitly addressed to the Haitian people" but signals a tension with "even though": the conclusion must contrast with that surface-level audience.

steps.title: Inventory the evidence
steps.content: The declaration is written in French (diplomatic language, not most Haitians' first language), reassures neighboring nations including the United States, and sets Haiti up as an example for future anti-colonial struggles. These features only make sense if the declaration was also speaking to outsiders, not just Haitians.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — aspects of the declaration were modeled on similar documents from other countries.: overreach — the passage never mentions any source documents the declaration was based on. ✗
  • Choice B — the French government may have been surprised by the declaration.: overreach — the passage says nothing about France's reaction. ✗
  • Choice C — many Haitian people opposed the revolution and the declaration.: overreach — no evidence about Haitian opposition appears in the text. ✗
  • Choice D — the declaration actually had several intended audiences.: matches the evidence (French language, US reassurance, message to future colonies) and resolves the "even though" contrast. ✓
D is correct.
